excerpt: Museum article reports a total city population of 700,000 at the time of the main bombing.
pages_or_sections: Article paragraph on 23 August bombing and delayed evacuation
confidence_note: Useful baseline for denominator modeling; not itself an exposed person-month estimate.

excerpt: By the 23 August main raid, only about 100,000 residents had been evacuated.
pages_or_sections: Article paragraph on 23 August bombing and delayed evacuation
confidence_note: Supports the exposed civilian baseline on the day the urban battle begins.

excerpt: Beevor's book description gives 40,000 civilian deaths from the first Richthofen air raids.
transform_note: Treated as a lower-bound anchor for episode-wide direct civilian deaths because later battle direct-death series are sparse.
confidence_note: Supporting lower bound, but not the strongest accessible page-level citation.

excerpt: Museum article reports up to 70,000 lives lost in the week-long bombing.
pages_or_sections: Article paragraph on the week-long bombing
transform_note: Used as the high anchor for direct civilian deaths because the article frames the number as contested.
confidence_note: Contested upper-bound style figure.

geographic_scope: Stalingrad city to east bank of the Volga
excerpt: Beevor's text states 300,000 civilians crossed to the east bank between 24 August and 10 September.
pages_or_sections: Chapter text on civilians trapped in Stalingrad
confidence_note: Used for denominator depletion rather than as a mortality series.

excerpt: Residual west-bank civilian presence after the main evacuation was above 50,000.
pages_or_sections: Chapter text on civilians trapped in Stalingrad
transform_note: Best value set slightly above the source floor because the text says 'well over' 50,000.
confidence_note: Important floor for late-summer civilian exposure.

excerpt: Late-battle survivors in the ruins were still above 10,000, implying a nonzero civilian denominator deep into winter.
pages_or_sections: Chapter text on civilians trapped in Stalingrad
transform_note: Best value set slightly above the floor because the source says 'over 10,000'.
confidence_note: Useful lower anchor for late-period civilian exposure.

excerpt: Table 9-1 gives 1,143,500 Soviet personnel at the beginning of the Stalingrad counteroffensive.
confidence_note: Strong strength snapshot, but broader than city-only combat footprint.

excerpt: Glantz notes 643,842 total Soviet casualties during the four months of the defensive operation.
pages_or_sections: Notes to pages 149–172; Table Q context
transform_note: Not used directly as deaths; it remains a casualty series that must be converted conservatively if used in modeling.
confidence_note: Good total-casualty anchor, but not a direct-death figure.

excerpt: Glantz's note gives the original Soviet strength for the defensive operation as 547,000.
confidence_note: Useful phase-A Soviet military denominator anchor.

victim_side: Axis armed forces in the Stalingrad pocket
excerpt: By late November Soviet forces had encircled 330,000 Axis personnel in the pocket.
confidence_note: Important late-November Axis denominator anchor.

victim_side: Axis armed forces in the Stalingrad region
excerpt: Glantz reports Rokossovsky's estimate of 600,000 Axis forces in the Stalingrad region at the counteroffensive start.
transform_note: Region-level estimate; used as an upper military denominator anchor, not as a city-only count.
confidence_note: Upper-bound style strength figure.

excerpt: Glantz states that Sixth Army was completely gone, with 147,000 dead and 91,000 POWs.
pages_or_sections: Narrative discussing the destruction of Sixth Army
transform_note: Used as the minimum Axis military death anchor; additional directly assigned Axis formations likely add deaths beyond Sixth Army.
confidence_note: Strong Axis death anchor but incomplete for the full operational theater.

excerpt: Table 9-2 gives 154,870 Soviet killed/captured in the offensive phase.
transform_note: Not treated as pure deaths because the category explicitly mixes killed with captured.
confidence_note: Useful upper-bound style component for Soviet direct-death modeling.

excerpt: Table 9-2 gives total Soviet offensive-phase casualties of 485,735.
transform_note: Serves as a casualty denominator for modeling a conservative Soviet death share.
confidence_note: Robust casualty figure, but not a death figure.

excerpt: World History Encyclopedia reports 736 civilians killed in the Moscow raids, with 3,513 wounded.
confidence_note: Useful direct-civilian numerator, but not enough to complete a city-matched DR.

excerpt: Dubson cites over 2.17 million evacuees from Moscow by 10 September 1941.
confidence_note: Shows denominator instability, but evacuation geography and remaining city population still need local reconstruction.

excerpt: Gulina reports official data that 2.8 million people were trapped in Leningrad at the siege outset.
confidence_note: Baseline civilian denominator context only.

excerpt: The relayed City Commission figure counts 16,747 residents killed in bombardments, separate from starvation totals.
pages_or_sections: Passage summarizing the Leningrad City Commission evidence
transform_note: Direct-bombardment series spans the full siege, so it is not directly phase-matched inside the bundle cutoff without further archival work.
confidence_note: Important direct-civilian series, but temporally broader than the current phase cut.

excerpt: The same City Commission summary reports 33,782 wounded in bombardments.
pages_or_sections: Passage summarizing the Leningrad City Commission evidence
transform_note: Kept separate from deaths, per project rules.
confidence_note: Auxiliary casualty context only.

excerpt: Dubson cites 774,876 evacuees from Leningrad by the end of 1941.
confidence_note: Important phase-1 denominator depletion anchor.

excerpt: Dubson gives a total of 1,814,151 evacuees from Leningrad during 1941–1943.
transform_note: Used only as a broad phase-2 displacement context indicator because the bundle ends on 1943-02-02.
confidence_note: Shows why a single siege denominator is misleading.
